pageSubtitle: A walkthrough from a system-simulation perspective: the clock, the queues, the resources, and how the metrics get built
title: 🎬 How a simulation run actually plays out
intro: The moment you click "Run simulation," a structured time game starts in the background: the simulation clock leaves 0 min, customers arrive one by one, they queue and get served across shared resources, until the configured duration runs out. This section is not a programming tutorial. It walks you through an entire run in the vocabulary of system simulation: how time advances, how entities and resources interact, and how the metrics you see at the end are built up along the way.
section1Concept: The least intuitive thing about discrete-event simulation (DES) is that the clock does not tick one second at a time. It is event-driven: it only advances when the next meaningful event fires. The internal structure holding "the next events and when they fire" is the Future Event List (FEL). Between any two events, system state is completely frozen, and no computation happens. That is why a 240-minute run finishes in under a second on your laptop: most of those "simulated minutes" contain no events, and the engine simply fast-forwards past them.
section1Model: In NekoServe, "meaningful events" include customer arrivals, patience timeouts, service completions, and cat rest endings. The engine never does busywork in the gaps. It jumps directly to the next scheduled event.
section1CodeNote: This line is not a loop over 240 ticks. It is "pop the next event from the FEL, jump the clock to its time, fire it", repeated until the clock reaches 240.
section2Title: 🚶 A customer is a process; resources are where queues form
section2Concept: In system simulation, every entity flowing through the system (here, a customer) is modelled as its own independent process, with its own state and its own next step. Many entities run concurrently, but they only interact through shared resources. Resources are where waiting actually happens: when a resource pool is at capacity, further requests automatically enter a FIFO queue until someone releases a slot.
section2Model: This model has three resource pools: seats, staff, and cats. Each pool has its own capacity and its own independent queue. A single customer passes through all three in sequence, experiencing a distinct wait at each one. When you see CUSTOMER_WAIT_SEAT or CUSTOMER_WAIT_CAT in the event log, that customer is currently stuck in one of these queues.
section2CodeNote: The first line declares a pool of 10 slots; the second line means "give me a slot, and if none is free, block here", and the yield itself is the queue.
section3Title: ⏱️ Every station has two durations: waiting and service
section3Concept: At each station, an entity experiences two distinct time segments: queueing time Wq, spent waiting for its turn, and service time Ws, spent actually being served. Recording them separately is what lets you spot bottlenecks: a long wait means the resource pool is too small, while a long service time means the service itself is slow. Total time in the system is W = Σ(Wq + Ws) across all stations. There is also a special case: if the customer has finite patience, they give up when their wait exceeds a threshold (queueing theory calls this reneging). Implementing it is a race between "service granted" and "patience exhausted", and whichever fires first wins.
section3Model: NekoServe tracks avgWaitForSeat, avgWaitForOrder, dining time, and interaction time separately for every customer, and the per-phase time chart on the Results page plots exactly these buckets. If a customer waits longer than maxWaitTime for a seat, a CUSTOMER_ABANDON event fires and they leave; that is the only branching path in the entire model.
section3CodeNote: This is the race in code: the | operator means "continue as soon as either side fires." If the seat is granted first, the customer sits down; if the patience timeout wins, .cancel() abandons the still-pending seat request.
section4Title: 🐱 Sub-processes and dynamic capacity: how cat rest is modelled
section4Concept: Real systems rarely have static capacity: machines fail, staff rotate, cats get tired. The standard trick for modelling "temporarily one fewer" is to remove a unit from the pool, spawn a sub-process that tracks when it comes back, and return it to the pool afterwards. The sub-process runs independently of the main flow, so it blocks nobody. Whether a cat should rest is itself a Bernoulli trial, a single random coin flip with success rate catRestProbability.
section4Model: This is why the cat pool uses SimPy's Container (which supports .get(n) / .put(n)) rather than a regular Resource. After an interaction, the engine flips the coin: on success, it fires a new "cat rest" sub-process; that cat does not return to the pool until a CAT_END_REST event fires. Meanwhile, the original customer has already walked out through CUSTOMER_LEAVE, so the rest happens in parallel.
section4CodeNote: This line spawns a new sub-process and does not wait for it; the customer leaves immediately, and the cat's rest runs on the side until it finally puts the unit back.
section5Title: 📊 From event log to metrics: what happens at the end of a run
section5Concept: A simulation's "result" is not computed at a single point in time; it accumulates as the run progresses. Every customer's wait time and every resource's busy minutes are written into lists and accumulators the moment those events happen. Only when the clock hits the configured duration and the engine stops does a single final aggregation turn those records into averages, utilizations, abandon rates, and so on. Every KPI on the Results page comes from this one end-of-run tally, and nothing is recomputed mid-run.
section5Model: wait_seat_times[] and stay_times[] are per-customer lists; total_seat_busy and total_staff_busy are running busy-time accumulators. After env.run() returns, core.py performs a handful of divisions to produce the MetricSummary, and hands it back (together with the full event log) to the Electron side for display.
section5CodeNote: The final aggregation is refreshingly plain. The x seatCount matters because multiple seats serve in parallel, so you must divide by "capacity x duration" to get the true average utilization.
section6Title: 🎲 One run is one coin flip: why replication matters
section6Concept: After the five steps above, you have a set of metrics. But those metrics are one random sample. Change the random seed and run again: every KPI will be different, because arrival times, service times, and cat behavior are all random variables. A single run might be unusually high or low, and you have no way to tell. The fix is replication: run the same parameters N times with different seeds and average the results. The more you run, the more the average stabilizes. This is not busywork; it is standard DES methodology.
section6Model: NekoServe's batch mode automatically runs with seed, seed+1, seed+2, ... in sequence. After all N runs finish, it collects the N MetricSummary objects and computes the mean and standard deviation for each KPI.
section6CodeNote: This is the core idea of replication: the outer loop changes the seed, the inner logic is the exact same simulation. Each env.run() is its own independent random world.
section7Title: 📏 Quantifying uncertainty: confidence intervals and parameter sweeps
section7Concept: Once you have the mean from N replications, the next question is: "how reliable is that mean?" A 95% confidence interval (CI) answers this. It means: if you re-ran the entire batch many times, 95% of those intervals would contain the true average. A narrow interval means your estimate is precise; a wide interval means N is too small and you need more replications. Another key tool is sensitivity analysis: hold everything fixed, change one parameter (e.g. staff count from 1 to 5), and watch how KPIs respond. This tells you "which parameter matters most," and is far more effective than adjusting blindly.
section7Model: NekoServe uses the t-distribution for CI computation because sample sizes are typically small (5 to 50). Sensitivity analysis runs a batch at each sweep point, and the result is displayed as a line chart with shaded CI bands.
section7CodeNote: The t-distribution automatically adjusts the critical value based on sample size: fewer samples mean a wider (more conservative) interval. Once N exceeds roughly 30, the t-value approaches 1.96, nearly identical to the normal distribution.
outro: From how time advances, to how entities queue, to how each station splits into waiting and service, to how capacity mutates dynamically, to how the final KPIs are assembled, to why replication matters, to how confidence intervals quantify uncertainty: that is the full life cycle of a simulation run, from execution to analysis. The next time you hit "Run simulation," replay these seven steps in your head; the KPIs on the Results page and the events in the Event Log will stop feeling like isolated numbers and start feeling like fragments of one continuous time game.
takeaway1: The engine only does work at event times. Everything between is fast-forwarded, which is why DES is so fast.
takeaway2: Entities never talk to each other directly. They only interact through shared resources and queues, which keeps the model clean.
takeaway3: yield req | timeout makes two events race. Whichever fires first determines the customer's fate.
takeaway4: A sub-process runs independently. The customer has already left; the cat rests on its own timeline.
takeaway5: Metrics accumulate event-by-event during the run. The final aggregation is just simple division.
takeaway6: Each run is one random sample. The more you run and average, the more stable the estimate.
takeaway7: A narrow CI means your estimate is precise. A wide CI means you need more replications.
friendlyIntro: When you hit "Run simulation," the app plays a fast-forwarded time game in the background: customers arrive one by one, grab seats, order food, hang out with cats, and leave. This page walks you through the rules of that game in plain language. No programming background needed.
friendlySection1Concept: Imagine you're watching a fast-forwarded movie. Boring moments with nothing happening get skipped. The film only pauses at important moments: "someone arrived," "food is ready," "customer left." That's exactly how the simulation clock works, which is why a 240-minute simulation finishes in under a second.
friendlySection1Model: In our cat cafe, the "important moments" are customer arrivals, patience running out, orders being ready, and cats waking up from naps. Everything in between is skipped entirely.
friendlySection2Concept: Each customer walks in and has to grab three things in order: a seat, a staff member (to order), and a cat (to play with). Each one has a limited supply. If they're all taken, you line up. The more people lining up, the longer the wait. Simple as that.
friendlySection2Model: Seats, staff, and cats each have their own separate queue. One customer passes through all three. When you see CUSTOMER_WAIT_SEAT in the event log, that customer is lining up for a seat.
friendlySection3Concept: Waiting has two parts: first you wait for your turn (standing in line), then you get served (it's your turn and things are happening). If the first part takes too long, the customer loses patience and leaves. It's like a race: does a seat open up first, or does the customer give up first?
friendlySection3Model: In this model, if a customer waits longer than the "max wait time" you configured, they trigger an abandon event and walk out. This is the only point where a customer can take a different path.
friendlySection4Concept: Cats don't stay with customers forever. After each interaction, the cat flips a coin to decide whether to take a nap. If it lands on rest, the cat goes to a corner and sleeps for a while, only coming back when the nap is over. During that time, there's one fewer cat available.
friendlySection4Model: The customer might already be gone by the time the cat is napping. They don't wait for each other at all. That's the "running independently" idea, just like real life.
friendlySection5Concept: The simulation doesn't wait until the end to calculate results. It keeps a running tally as it goes: every customer's wait time and every seat's busy minutes are written down the moment they happen. When the clock stops, one round of simple arithmetic produces all the final numbers.
friendlySection5Model: Every KPI you see on the Results page comes from this running tally. One final calculation when the run ends gives you everything.
friendlySection6Concept: Running the simulation once is like rolling a die once. The result is different every time, because arrival times, dining durations, and cat behavior are all random. A single result might be unusually high or low. The fix: run it many times and average the results. Like a survey: the more people you ask, the more reliable the answer.
friendlySection6Model: Batch mode automatically runs N times, each with a different random seed. After all runs finish, it averages the results for a more stable answer.
friendlySection7Concept: After running many times, you get an average. But how reliable is that average? The error band answers this: a narrow band means the answer is precise and stable; a wide band means you haven't run enough times yet.
friendlySection7Model: The small text below each KPI card shows the error band. If it's wide, try increasing replications to 20 or 30.
friendlyTakeaway1: Boring moments get skipped, so the simulation is super fast!
friendlyTakeaway2: Customers don't interact with each other. They're all just competing for seats and staff.
friendlyTakeaway3: The queue timer and the patience timer count down at the same time. Whichever hits zero first wins.
friendlyTakeaway4: The customer leaves, the cat naps on its own. Neither waits for the other.
friendlyTakeaway5: The simulation keeps a running tally, and one round of arithmetic at the end gives you all the results.
friendlyTakeaway6: Like a survey: the more people you ask, the more stable the result!
friendlyTakeaway7: Narrow error band = stable answer. Wide band = need more runs.
friendlyOutro: Fast-forward clock, lining up for resources, patience races, coin-flip cat naps, running tallies, averaging multiple runs, and checking error bands. That's what every simulation does from start to finish. Next time you hit "Run simulation," try replaying these seven steps in your head!

ciExplorer.narrowHint: Interval is narrow, estimate is precise
ciExplorer.wideHint: Interval is wide, need more replications
ciExplorer.friendlyHint: Try it! Drag N higher and watch the band shrink~
